Abstract

This paper presents a method for autonomous driving scene understanding. We propose a multi-modal fusion architecture that combines LiDAR and camera inputs. Our approach achieves strong performance on standard benchmarks.

Introduction

Autonomous driving requires robust perception under diverse conditions. Recent work has explored transformer-based architectures for this task. However, real-time deployment remains challenging due to computational constraints. We address this gap with a lightweight attention mechanism.

Method

Our architecture consists of three main components. First, a feature extractor processes camera images. Second, a point cloud encoder handles LiDAR data. Third, a fusion module combines both feature streams. The fusion module uses cross-attention to align modalities. Training is performed on the nuScenes dataset with standard augmentation.

Abstract

We study the problem of multi-agent reinforcement learning for cooperative navigation. The key challenge is scaling to many agents while maintaining stable training. Our method introduces a communication protocol that reduces bandwidth requirements. Experimental results show improvements over strong baselines.

Introduction

Multi-agent systems appear in many real-world settings. Traffic control, warehouse automation, and surveillance are examples. Scaling RL to many agents is non-trivial. Centralized training with decentralized execution has become standard.

IEEE Transactions on Robotics, Vol. 42, No. 3, 2026
Proceedings of the International Conference on Robotics

Method

Our approach uses a graph neural network to model agent interactions. Each node represents an agent and edges represent communication channels. The GNN is trained end-to-end with the policy network.

Abstract

Digital twins enable simulation-based evaluation of cyber-physical systems. We present a framework for verifying safety properties in digital twin models. The framework supports both bounded and unbounded temporal logic specifications. We demonstrate our approach on an automotive case study.

Introduction

Digital twins have gained attention in industry and academia. They provide a virtual replica of a physical system that can be used for analysis. However, formal verification of digital twin properties is underexplored. We fill this gap.

Method

Our framework translates digital twin models into timed automata. The translation preserves the semantics of the original model. We use the UPPAAL model checker for verification. The translation has been implemented as a prototype tool.

2

Method continued

The prototype supports a subset of the digital twin modeling language. We discuss limitations in the evaluation section. The tool is available as open source software.

Abstract

We investigate the robustness of deep neural net-
works against adversarial perturbations. Our analysis covers both white-
box and black-
box threat models. We propose a defense based on random-
ized smoothing that provides certified bounds. The defense is effective across multiple archi-
tectures and datasets.

Introduction

Adversarial examples have received significant atten-
tion in the machine learning community. Small, impercep-
tible perturbations to inputs can cause large changes in predic-
tions. Defending against such attacks is an open problem. Certi-
fied defenses offer guarantees but often reduce accuracy.

Method

Our approach builds on the random-
ized smoothing frame-
work. We add Gaussian noise to inputs before feeding them to the base classifier. The smoothed classifier is the most likely prediction under this noise distribu-
tion. We derive new bounds for the multi-
class setting.

Introduction

Policy simulation models are widely used to evaluate the potential impact of regulatory interventions before they are implemented in practice. These models must balance fidelity to real-world dynamics with computational tractability, since overly detailed models become infeasible to calibrate while overly simplified models may miss critical nonlinearities. In this work we propose a hybrid approach that combines agent-based modeling with system dynamics to capture both micro-level heterogeneity and macro-level feedback loops. Our framework allows practitioners to specify policies at a high level of abstraction while the underlying simulation handles the complexity of agent interactions and emergent system behavior. We evaluate the framework on a case study involving carbon pricing policy where we compare outcomes under different revenue recycling schemes including lump-sum rebates, payroll tax cuts, and green technology subsidies. The results show that the choice of revenue recycling mechanism has a substantial effect on both economic efficiency and distributional outcomes, with lump-sum rebates favoring low-income households and tax cuts favoring high-income deciles. We also analyze sensitivity to key parameters including the elasticity of substitution between labor and capital, the rate of technical progress in clean energy, and the discount rate applied to future damages. The framework is implemented as an open-source Python package that integrates with standard calibration tooling and provides visualization utilities for exploring the policy landscape.

Results

Table 1: Comparison of methods on benchmark datasets.

Method        | Dataset A | Dataset B | Dataset C | Avg.
--------------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------
Baseline      |    72.3   |    68.1   |    75.4   | 71.9
Method-X      |    78.9   |    74.2   |    80.1   | 77.7
Ours          |    82.4   |    79.6   |    84.3   | 82.1
Ours+aug      |    84.1   |    81.2   |    85.7   | 83.7

Table 2: Ablation study results.

Component     | Accuracy | F1    | Time (ms)
--------------|----------|-------|----------
Full model    |   84.1   | 83.2  |   42
No attention  |   80.3   | 79.5  |   31
No fusion     |   76.8   | 75.9  |   28

Discussion

The results demonstrate that each component contributes to overall performance. Removing the attention module reduces accuracy by 3.8 points on average. Removing the fusion module has a larger effect, reducing accuracy by 7.3 points. The runtime overhead of the full model is modest at 42 milliseconds per inference.

Method

Our model processes input through three stages. Figure 1 illustrates the overall architecture. Each stage is described below.

Figure 1: Overview of the proposed three-stage architecture. Stage 1 performs feature extraction, stage 2 performs cross-modal fusion, and stage 3 produces the final prediction.

Stage 1 extracts features using a pre-trained backbone. Stage 2 fuses features from multiple modalities. Stage 3 produces the final classification output.

Results

We evaluate on three datasets. Figure 2 shows the precision-recall curves. Table 1 summarizes the numerical results.

Figure 2: Precision-recall curves on the three evaluation datasets. Our method achieves higher precision at all recall levels compared to baselines.

Table 1: Numerical results across datasets. Our method outperforms all baselines.

Our method achieves 84.1 percent accuracy on Dataset A, 81.2 percent on Dataset B, and 85.7 percent on Dataset C. The improvements are statistically significant with p-value less than 0.01.

Introduction

Reinforcement learning has been applied to robotics extensively [1, 2, 3]. Early work by Sutton and Barto [4] established the foundational framework. More recent methods [5, 6, 7] have scaled RL to high-dimensional control. Levine et al. [8] demonstrated end-to-end training of visuo-motor policies. Schulman et al. [9] introduced PPO which has become a standard algorithm.

Method

Our method builds on the actor-critic framework [10, 11]. We use the generalized advantage estimator [12] for variance reduction. The policy network architecture follows [13] with modifications. Optimization uses Adam [14] with learning rate scheduling. The reward function design is inspired by [15, 16, 17]. We also incorporate curriculum learning [18] to stabilize training.

References

[1] Kober et al., 2013. Reinforcement learning in robotics.
[2] Peters and Schaal, 2008. Reinforcement learning of motor skills.
[3] Deisenroth et al., 2013. A survey on policy search.
[4] Sutton and Barto, 2018. Reinforcement learning: An introduction.
[5] Haarnoja et al., 2018. Soft actor-critic.
[6] Fujimoto et al., 2018. Addressing function approximation error.
[7] Lillicrap et al., 2016. Continuous control with deep RL.
[8] Levine et al., 2016. End-to-end training of deep visuo-motor policies.
[9] Schulman et al., 2017. Proximal policy optimization algorithms.
[10] Konda and Tsitsiklis, 2003. On actor-critic algorithms.
[11] Mnih et al., 2016. Asynchronous methods for deep RL.
[12] Schulman et al., 2016. High-dimensional continuous control using GAE.
